transcript.transcript: Uh, yeah. My operating principle has always been that I do not, and will not allow anyone to live in a house that I don't want to live in myself. I would like to see the day in this community and in our country, when everyone will have an opportunity to live in a very decent environment. I went to the Greater Hartford Development Fund and I asked them would they be willing to loan the City of Hartford $100,000? This $100,000 would be in cases where a landlord has refused to make any improvements on his property, including both the interior and the exterior of the building. As you know, and I am sure you have seen them. They are in many apartments here where it's rundown, where it's in very bad shape, and the landlords have refused to make the necessary corrections. Now the City of Hartford has taken these people to court in some cases. It's a long, drawn out matter, and I have attempted to bring about housing courts in in the city of Hartford. I have been I have not been successful because this has to come from the state legislature. And, um, uh, I have appealed to the powers there, and nothing has been done because we don't have the power on the city council to do so. However, under this plan, we, the city of Hartford, would borrow this money. If, for instance, you own property at, let's say, number so and so street, and after sending you a series of letters, you refuse to make the necessary repairs. We would come in, do the repairs and put a lien on your property bearing the same interest rates that we borrowed the money. So the City of Hartford is not putting up any money, and we are performing a service for people. There seems to be a preponderance of West Indian origin people involved in real estate. Is there any particular reason for this pattern? Well, I guess, um, first, there is a number of people truly who got themselves involved in the whole question of real estate. And I think the reason for that is simply that, um, in these other communities where we lived, they, they push has always been to own your own and not rent. And I would hope that the day will come in this community when I when efforts will be made to at least let a man even own his own apartment instead of renting. You see, we are always going to have rental apartments, but the trust should be home ownership. And that is why under the old concept of condominium housing, uh, condominium apartment living, a man has a piece of the action. And this is what I'd like to see, uh, in the city of Hartford. Is there any is there any particular reason why, uh, any attitude that some people have which makes it better or which makes it makes them more able to cope with a situation like West Indian people? Well, I think that, um, in Hartford, you will find that many of the homes are really owned by people from the South, too. And, uh, they want a lot of houses in Hartford. And I guess years ago in Hartford, I, I remember talking to Doctor Simpson, who has lived in Hartford 25, 30 years ago. Uh, many people who came from the South and lived in Hartford, they owned their properties. And I think that, um, that what you're seeing now here in Hartford is this. If the houses were available, thousands of people who now live in Hartford, here right now, would be able to own their own home under the federal programs that are now available. Uh, the the trees, the, um, 235 and so on and so forth. These are tools that now make it easier for someone to get a job, but to, uh, get a house. But you do have to have the houses being built, and we are not getting them built as rapidly as we should. Do you feel you're successful and effective as a councilman? Do you feel like your democracy is working for you? I believe in the democratic system. Uh, truly. Um, and, uh, my old background spells that out. When I came to live in Hartford again, I pointed out that it was a lot of poor people giving their hand. A few middle class people, and I met some rich, but to some poor people who gave me many assistance. I believe in the democratic system because. And I believe the opportunity, uh, can be realized if all of us are willing to commit, uh, the, the, the resources of this country into the rebuilding of of of the cities. And I'm talking directly about the cities. If we were to put money in to build decent housing, good educational facilities. I'm not talking about mediocre educational system. I'm putting in the dollars and the equipment to do the job. That will help stimulate the mind. I think that, uh, you will see thousands and thousands of our people blossoming out. One of the observations that I have made is that it seems like there's a lot of fragmentation in the community, especially on the black side, to be blunt. Well, I, I can answer your question by saying this. There is fragmentation, but I think with the kind of leadership that we have developed over the past 4 or 5 years that you will see in the years to come, greater unity. I think you will see greater resolve to work together. And this is just growing pains that we are going through right at the moment. Really. You're very optimistic person. I if you knew how far this community has come from in terms of working towards some of these goals, then you will be as as I am convinced that the future for all of us holds, that the future holds great promise for us. Yet what about the evidence of anger that one sees on the street. Yes, there is anger. And this anger can be transformed, I believe. Uh, into, um. Into. Constructive, uh, uh, development. How? How? By fragmentation. Well, it means it can be healed again if we develop as quickly as we can. Decent. Are you. Are you talking to the South side now? No, I'm talking to all of us in the city. I'm talking about all of us in here. It can be healed if all of us. I'm talking about all the people in the city. If all of us work together to rebuild the city in terms of making sure that every person in Hartford lives in a very decent home, that there are good educational institutions here in Hartford, that other training programs are developed to train those that are untrained, and that better avenues of communication are being developed in the community. These avenues are sometimes not as what they should be because we don't speak to each other. We don't get a chance to laugh with each other as we should. And I think when these areas of communication are opened up and they are opening up, and I think when we start pushing more in these areas that are that I would like when I say pushing, I mean doing things. We as the elected officials are saying to people, look, you're going to be able to live in a decent home next year and be able to deliver that. Then these are violet days or this violent era will come to an end, and we will be able to have an era of tranquility and understanding. And I want tranquility, and therefore I am going to do all I can along with you in trying to bring about the changes for the better. The key. The key word to me seems to be, uh, delivery. Yes, the word is deliver. And all of what I have said to you will be hollow words if I can't deliver.

lines.line: # Personal Housing Principles and City Intervention Plan

> The speaker outlines a personal commitment to ensuring that no one lives in substandard housing, expressing a vision for universal access to decent living environments. They describe a proposal to secure a $100,000 loan for the City of Hartford to address negligent landlords who refuse to maintain their properties. The plan involves the city making necessary repairs and placing a lien on the property, ensuring that the city is not financially burdened while providing a vital service to residents. The speaker also notes the challenges of legal action against landlords and the limitations of city power without state legislative support.

metadata.title: Personal Housing Principles and City Intervention Plan
metadata.synopsis: The speaker outlines a personal commitment to ensuring that no one lives in substandard housing, expressing a vision for universal access to decent living environments. They describe a proposal to secure a $100,000 loan for the City of Hartford to address negligent landlords who refuse to maintain their properties. The plan involves the city making necessary repairs and placing a lien on the property, ensuring that the city is not financially burdened while providing a vital service to residents. The speaker also notes the challenges of legal action against landlords and the limitations of city power without state legislative support.

lines.line: > The discussion shifts to the notable involvement of West Indian individuals in real estate, which the speaker attributes to cultural values emphasizing homeownership over renting. The speaker expresses hope that more people in the community will have opportunities to own their homes, referencing the benefits of condominium living. They also highlight that many Southern migrants in Hartford have historically owned property, and that federal programs now make homeownership more accessible, though the supply of new housing is insufficient to meet demand.

metadata.title: Cultural Attitudes Toward Homeownership
metadata.synopsis: The discussion shifts to the notable involvement of West Indian individuals in real estate, which the speaker attributes to cultural values emphasizing homeownership over renting. The speaker expresses hope that more people in the community will have opportunities to own their homes, referencing the benefits of condominium living. They also highlight that many Southern migrants in Hartford have historically owned property, and that federal programs now make homeownership more accessible, though the supply of new housing is insufficient to meet demand.

lines.line: # Belief in Democracy and the Need for Urban Investment

> When asked about their effectiveness and faith in democracy, the speaker affirms a strong belief in the democratic system, shaped by personal experiences of support from various socioeconomic groups. They argue that true opportunity can be realized if the nation commits resources to rebuilding cities, particularly through investments in quality housing and education. The speaker envisions a future where such investments enable people to reach their full potential.

metadata.title: Belief in Democracy and the Need for Urban Investment
metadata.synopsis: When asked about their effectiveness and faith in democracy, the speaker affirms a strong belief in the democratic system, shaped by personal experiences of support from various socioeconomic groups. They argue that true opportunity can be realized if the nation commits resources to rebuilding cities, particularly through investments in quality housing and education. The speaker envisions a future where such investments enable people to reach their full potential.

lines.line: # Community Fragmentation and Hope for Unity

> Addressing concerns about fragmentation within the community, particularly among Black residents, the speaker acknowledges the issue but expresses optimism that recent leadership developments will foster greater unity in the coming years. They describe the current situation as 'growing pains' and suggest that increased collaboration and resolve will lead to stronger community bonds.

metadata.title: Community Fragmentation and Hope for Unity
metadata.synopsis: Addressing concerns about fragmentation within the community, particularly among Black residents, the speaker acknowledges the issue but expresses optimism that recent leadership developments will foster greater unity in the coming years. They describe the current situation as 'growing pains' and suggest that increased collaboration and resolve will lead to stronger community bonds.

lines.line: > The conversation turns to visible anger in the community, which the speaker believes can be redirected into constructive development. They stress the importance of collective efforts to provide decent housing, quality education, and job training, as well as improving communication among residents. The speaker emphasizes that healing and tranquility are possible if everyone works together to address these fundamental needs, and that elected officials must be able to deliver on promises to foster trust and peace.

metadata.title: Transforming Anger and Building Community
metadata.synopsis: The conversation turns to visible anger in the community, which the speaker believes can be redirected into constructive development. They stress the importance of collective efforts to provide decent housing, quality education, and job training, as well as improving communication among residents. The speaker emphasizes that healing and tranquility are possible if everyone works together to address these fundamental needs, and that elected officials must be able to deliver on promises to foster trust and peace.

lines.line: > The speaker agrees that the ability to deliver tangible results is crucial, stating that all their previous statements would be meaningless without actual follow-through. They underscore the importance of fulfilling commitments to the community, reinforcing that real change depends on the delivery of promised improvements.

metadata.title: The Necessity of Delivering Results
metadata.synopsis: The speaker agrees that the ability to deliver tangible results is crucial, stating that all their previous statements would be meaningless without actual follow-through. They underscore the importance of fulfilling commitments to the community, reinforcing that real change depends on the delivery of promised improvements.

story.title: Colin Bennet realtor speaks
story.description: The interview focuses on housing and community development issues in Hartford, emphasizing the importance of living in decent environments and promoting homeownership over renting. The interviewee discusses challenges with landlords neglecting property maintenance and outlines a plan for the city to borrow money for repairs by imposing liens on properties. Additionally, the involvement of West Indian and Southern communities in real estate is explored. The interviewee expresses optimism about future community unity and emphasizes the need for better housing, education, and communication within the city. Delivery of promises is highlighted as crucial for future progress.
